examples.title: Defining ER Diagram Components
examples.description: Basic example showing how to represent ER diagram entities, attributes, and relationships using Python data structures
examples.explanation: This basic example demonstrates how to represent ER diagram components (entities, attributes, relationships, cardinality) using Python dictionaries. Students learn to identify: (1) Strong vs Weak entities, (2) Primary keys (PK), (3) Relationship cardinality (1:1, 1:N, M:N), and (4) Structural constraints. This is fundamental to understanding ER notation before moving to visualization.

examples.title: ER Diagram Visualization with Graphviz
examples.description: Intermediate example demonstrating how to programmatically generate and visualize an ER diagram using the Graphviz library
examples.code:
# ER Diagram Notation - Visual Generation using Graphviz # For BSc Physical Science (CS) - Delhi University from graphviz import Digraph # Create ER Diagram er_diagram = Digraph('ER_Diagram', format='png') er_diagram.attr(rankdir='LR') # Left to Right layout er_diagram.attr('node', shape='box', style='rounded,filled', fontname='Arial') er_diagram.attr('edge', fontname='Arial') # Add entities (rectangles) # Syntax: Entity [label="EntityName\nattribute1\nattribute2"] er_diagram.node('Student', label='<\n\n\n\n\n\n
Student
student_id (PK)
name
email
phone
>', shape='plain') er_diagram.node('Course', label='<\n\n\n\n\n
Course
course_id (PK)
course_name
credits
>', shape='plain') er_diagram.node('Instructor', label='<\n\n\n\n\n
Instructor
instructor_id (PK)
name
specialization
>', shape='plain') # Add weak entity er_diagram.node('Enrollment', label='<\n\n\n\n\n
Enrollment (Weak)
enrollment_id (PK)
grade
semester
>', shape='plain') # Add relationships with cardinality notation # Diamond shapes represented as nodes er_diagram.node('teaches', label='teaches', shape='diamond', style='filled', fillcolor='lightyellow') er_diagram.node('enrolls', label='enrolls\n(M:N)', shape='diamond', style='filled', fillcolor='lightyellow') # Connect entities to relationships # Notation: Entity -- Relationship [label="cardinality"] er_diagram.edge('Instructor', 'teaches', label='(1,N)', dir='both') er_diagram.edge('Course', 'teaches', label='(1,1)', dir='both') er_diagram.edge('Student', 'enrolls', label='(1,N)', dir='both') er_diagram.edge('Course', 'enrolls', label='(1,N)', dir='both') er_diagram.edge('Student', 'Enrollment', label='(1,1)', dir='both') er_diagram.edge('Course', 'Enrollment', label='(1,1)', dir='both') # Add title er_diagram.attr(label='\nUniversity Database ER Diagram\n', fontsize='20', fontname='Arial Bold') # Save and render output_path = er_diagram.render('er_diagram_output') print(f"✅ ER Diagram generated successfully!") print(f"📁 Output file: {output_path}") print("\nER Diagram Key Notations Used:") print(" □ Rectangles → Strong Entities") print(" □ Dashed Box → Weak Entity") print(" ◇ Diamonds → Relationships") print(" (1,1) → Exactly one (total participation)") print(" (1,N) → One to many") print(" (M,N) → Many to many")
examples.explanation: This intermediate example shows how to generate a visual ER diagram programmatically using Graphviz. It demonstrates key ER notation symbols: (1) Rectangles for entities, (2) Dashed rectangles for weak entities, (3) Diamond shapes for relationships, (4) Cardinality notation (1:1, 1:N, M:N) on relationship lines, and (5) Total vs partial participation. Run this code to generate an actual PNG image of an ER diagram.

examples.title: ER Notation Parser and Validator
examples.description: Advanced example that parses entity-relationship definitions and validates ER diagram notation rules
examples.code:
# ER Diagram Notation - Parser and Validator
# For BSc Physical Science (CS) - Delhi University

import re
from typing import Dict, List, Tuple

class ERDiagramValidator:
    """Validates ER diagram notation and constraints"""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.entities = {}
        self.relationships = []
        self.errors = []
        self.warnings = []
    
    def add_entity(self, name: str, attributes: List[str], is_weak: bool = False):
        """Add entity with notation validation"""
        # Check for primary key
        pk_found = any('(pk)' in attr.lower() or 'primary key' in attr.lower() 
                      for attr in attributes)
        
        if not pk_found and not is_weak:
            self.warnings.append(f"Entity '{name}' has no Primary Key defined")
        
        # Check attribute naming conventions
        for attr in attributes:
            if attr[0].isupper():
                self.errors.append(f"Attribute '{attr}' in '{name}' should be lowercase")
        
        self.entities[name] = {
            'attributes': attributes,
            'is_weak': is_weak,
            'pk_found': pk_found
        }
    
    def add_relationship(self, name: str, entities: List[str], 
                        cardinality: str, participation: str = 'partial'):
        """Add relationship with notation validation"""
        # Validate participating entities exist
        for entity in entities:
            if entity not in self.entities:
                self.errors.append(f"Entity '{entity}' in relationship '{name}' not defined")
        
        # Validate cardinality notation
        valid_cardinalities = ['1:1', '1:n', '1:n', 'n:m', 'm:n', 
                              'one-to-one', 'one-to-many', 'many-to-many']
        if cardinality.lower() not in valid_cardinalities:
            self.errors.append(f"Invalid cardinality '{cardinality}' in relationship '{name}'")
        
        self.relationships.append({
            'name': name,
            'entities': entities,
            'cardinality': cardinality,
            'participation': participation
        })
    
    def validate_weak_entity(self, weak_entity: str, identifying_rel: str):
        """Validate weak entity notation rules"""
        if weak_entity not in self.entities:
            self.errors.append(f"Weak entity '{weak_entity}' not defined")
            return
        
        if not self.entities[weak_entity]['is_weak']:
            self.errors.append(f"Entity '{weak_entity}' marked as weak but not declared weak")
        
        # Weak entity should have partial key (discriminator)
        print(f"  ✓ Weak entity '{weak_entity}' must have partial key (discriminator)")
        print(f"  ✓ Identified through '{identifying_rel}' relationship")
    
    def validate_total_participation(self, entity: str, relationship: str):
        """Validate total participation constraint (double line notation)"""
        for rel in self.relationships:
            if rel['name'] == relationship and entity in rel['entities']:
                if rel['participation'] == 'total':
                    print(f"  ✓ Total participation: {entity} -- {relationship} (double line)")
    
    def generate_notation_summary(self) -> str:
        """Generate ER notation summary report"""
        summary = []
        summary.append("=" * 60)
        summary.append("ER DIAGRAM NOTATION VALIDATION REPORT")
        summary.append("=" * 60)
        
        summary.append("\n📋 ENTITIES:")
        for name, data in self.entities.items():
            weak_tag = " (Weak)" if data['is_weak'] else ""
            pk_tag = " ✓ PK" if data['pk_found'] else " ✗ No PK"
            summary.append(f"  □ {name}{weak_tag} - {pk_tag}")
            for attr in data['attributes']:
                summary.append(f"     ├── {attr}")
        
        summary.append("\n🔗 RELATIONSHIPS:")
        for rel in self.relationships:
            summary.append(f"  ◇ {rel['name']}")
            summary.append(f"     ├── Entities: {', '.join(rel['entities'])}")
            summary.append(f"     ├── Cardinality: {rel['cardinality']}")
            summary.append(f"     └── Participation: {rel['participation']}")
        
        return "\n".join(summary)
    
    def validate_all(self) -> bool:
        """Run all validations and return status"""
        print("\n🔍 VALIDATION RULES APPLIED:")
        print("  1. Primary Key existence for strong entities")
        print("  2. Attribute naming conventions (lowercase)")
        print("  3. Entity existence in relationships")
        print("  4. Valid cardinality notation")
        print("  5. Weak entity identification rules")
        
        print("\n⚠️  WARNINGS:")
        for w in self.warnings:
            print(f"  {w}")
        
        print("\n❌ ERRORS:")
        for e in self.errors:
            print(f"  {e}")
        
        return len(self.errors) == 0


# ============================================
# DEMONSTRATION: University Database ER Diagram
# ============================================

validator = ERDiagramValidator()

# Define Entities with notation
validator.add_entity("Student", ["student_id (PK)", "name", "email", "dob"], is_weak=False)
validator.add_entity("Course", ["course_id (PK)", "title", "credits"], is_weak=False)
validator.add_entity("Enrollment", ["enrollment_id", "grade", "semester"], is_weak=True)
validator.add_entity("Department", ["dept_id (PK)", "dept_name"], is_weak=False)

# Define Relationships with cardinality notation
validator.add_relationship("enrolls", ["Student", "Course"], "M:N", "partial")
validator.add_relationship("has", ["Student", "Enrollment"], "1:N", "total")
validator.add_relationship("belongs_to", ["Course", "Department"], "1:N", "total")

# Validate weak entity
validator.validate_weak_entity("Enrollment", "has")

# Validate total participation
validator.validate_total_participation("Student", "has")
validator.validate_total_participation("Course", "belongs_to")

# Generate report
print(validator.generate_notation_summary())

# Final validation
is_valid = validator.validate_all()
print(f"\n{'✅ ER Diagram is VALID' if is_valid else '❌ ER Diagram has ERRORS'}")
examples.explanation: This advanced example creates a comprehensive ER diagram validator that checks proper notation usage. It demonstrates: (1) Primary key notation (PK), (2) Weak entity identification, (3) Cardinality notation validation (1:1, 1:N, M:N), (4) Total vs partial participation (double line vs single line), (5) Attribute naming conventions, and (6) Entity-relationship integrity. This is highly relevant for DBMS students to verify their ER diagrams follow standard notation before converting to relational schema.
